speaker: interviewer
text: February 25, 1997. This interview is with Aniela Radek, R-A-D-E-K, nee Kosturek, K-O-S-T-U-R-E-K. My name is of Aviva Chernick, C-H-E-R-N-I-C-K. We are near Toronto, Ontario, Canada. This interview will take place in English. Today's date is February  25, 1997. This interview is with Aniela Radek nee Kosturek. My name is Aviva Chernick. We are in Toronto, Ontario, Canada. This interview will take place in English. Please state your name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Ania Radek.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ania is A-N-I-A. That's the way people call me. Radek-- R-A-D-E-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is Radek your name from birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my name from birth is Kosturek-- K-O-S-T-U-R-E-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: My birth date is December 8, 1926.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Poland in Glo-- in Tryncza and lived later on in Glogowiec.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were the n-- what-- what were your parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents' names were Joseph and Mary.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your mother's maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Maria Wlazlo, Wlazlo, W-L-A-Z-L-O.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any siblings?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have one brother Augustin. And he is two years older than, than I am.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where you lived in Tryncza, did anybody else live in the house with you besides your parents and your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's mother lived there. She was para-- paralyzed. And she just stayed in bed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Caroline was her name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is that-- was that all the people who were in the house with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my brother and my mother. In the beginning, it was my father who left me when I was one year old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before, before he left, what kind of work did he do to support this family, your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was just working as a farm help going from one place to another and bringing some food here and there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he work long days?

speaker: interviewee
text: He worked the usual days. As you know, on the farm, people do work from early morning till late at night. And he also had to go to military, because his military service was due when he was married. So he was hardly at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: When he was-- when he was at home, what other kinds of activities did he do besides his work?

speaker: interviewee
text: His only activities were gardening and helping other people, working on the farm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he a religious man?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say he was hanging in between.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, and what kind of person was your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was a very kind, very happy person, a very hardworking person, and most of all, she was obedient in a way that is offensive to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she work also?

speaker: interviewee
text: She had to-- oh, yes, she worked part time, but she, she had to take care of me and my brother and of this lady, of her mo-- stepmother. So she had too much work there already.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she-- what kind of religion did your mother have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Roman Catholic.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she observant?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was very much observant, but she also knew how to enjoy herself and going to dances and so on. And life was-- when she was young-- was a happy time for her while she was young.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you brought up religiously?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much so-- very much. I heard nothing but holy scripts. And I heard nothing but people coming from church, and talking about what they heard in the church all the time, and, and everything that was supposed to be our, our most necessary thing for salvation. And I believe that, yes, at times, it was a bit too much. But what can we do?

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of school did you go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in Poland, I only finished elementary grades. War came when I was 13.

speaker: interviewer
text: So before, before 13, did you go to a public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Public school. I went to public school. And I was quite a good student. My, my brother as well, he went to public school. But we had too many chores. So between school and the chores, we didn't have much time. We didn't have time, for example, to enjoy ourselves or to play. We didn't have any toys because that was not necessary for salvation. [LAUGHS] So that, that was the way, you know, in the family that brought us-- my grandma and my two aunts, Frania and Stephania and my Uncle Piotr. Those people lived in Glogowiec where I and my brother had to be transferred because my parents emigrated to France.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when your parents emigrated to France?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was two years old. And my brother was four. My father went first when I was one year old. And then he brought his wife. And that was the end of my parents. And I didn't know them until I was 18.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did they leave? Why did they emigrate to France?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe, like a lot of people did, to look for a better life, living conditions. And they were hoping that eventually they might bring their children there as well. But they never did. When the war came, and they didn't even know that we were in the camps. And it was a difficult time for most people-- for us, because we were a burden to our protectors, our benefactors.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who, who-- with whom did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived with my grandmother and aunts and uncle, as I mentioned previously.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was in which town?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Glogowiec. Glogowiec is about four kilometers from Tryncza and a place that is, well, a godforsaken village. There, there is not even a highway going through-- between two rivers in the corner, just in that triangle. And we didn't know much about the war. Nor did I know much about the war at all. I only went to Sieniawa. Sieniawa was a Jewish [INAUDIBLE] city-- most inhabitants were Jewish-- where we went shopping sometimes with my, my aunts or my neighbor.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was familiar then at that time with the Jewish people. And what really struck me, and what I'm going-- what I wrote- I am writing right now in my book is the fact that people were not very kind to Jewish people. And I always ask myself, why? And it was-- it hurt me all the time. And I was asking them if you are so Christian, why do you call those people names? Or why do you hate them?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they had all kinds of little excuses. But they could not stand up to me. I just couldn't understand that. I had-- I remember this encounter of this very lovely gentlemen. He was coming to our village. And he was-- he was buying horses and bringing some special things for sale. And, and one day I was going on a pilgrimage. I was six then. My aunt was going on the pilgrimage to a special place. And I hurt my leg on the way. It was about 16 kilometers of walking. I couldn't walk anymore. And this gentlemen-- this old man, a Jewish man-- was coming back from somewhere, and they asked him to take me back home. And he sat me on the-- on his wagon-- on his special wagon where he had all his stuff for sale and, and a little horse, a grey, grey mare. And we was-- I was very afraid with him because he looked rather forbidding. He had a grey beard and, and those very intensely dark eyes and staring at me. And he's-- he was asking me what was-- what was my name. And I told him. And I said- and then I asked him what was his name. And I don't remember to this day what was his name. I was so afraid. And he gave me some cookies, on a-- which he had with him, because he bought them in [NON-ENGLISH]. And we started to really becoming very good friends. And he was joking. And that was, for me, a really a great confirmation that the whole world could be beautiful if we all would love one another. And I believe what I'm saying right now was the same all to my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ania, I'm just going to-- I'm just going to take you back a bit. Did you notice any specific events of anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I didn't notice them so much because I was mostly staying in, in that village. I didn't know much about it-- just people talking. I only heard it-- well, we will probably come to it a bit later when I would say-- talk about the start of the war, where I had some very drastic encounters. And it really-- I would like to tell you this story.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in Glogowiec, where you went to school, was it-- what kind of children were at the school with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were mostly children like I was myself-- very nice kids, I mean, nice--

speaker: interviewer
text: What religious denomination were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only Christian, only Catholic. There was not-- that was-- there were about, I don't know, 25 families in the whole village. And they were of the-- all of the same thing, except for my friend, my very best friend, Helen. Her mother was-- had her out of wedlock. Her mother was working for this one-- only this one unique lady in our village who was-- her name was-- everybody called her Chaya. And she was admirable person. I loved her. And Helen called her mamcia. Mamcia means my little mother. Even though-- her, her mother she called mamusia, my, my mother. And she called her mamcia. And this lady was very educated. She had all kinds of books. And she lived in the center of the village-- on the crossroads, so to speak, of the village. And what I was-- what, what I going to mention it was the bad part to that. Sometimes kids were throwing mud balls at her windows. And the teacher was, of course, telling them not to do that. That is very bad, so on, so on. So she had those shades, especially made of wood, wooden shades, to, to cover her windows. She was a very, very nice lady. And she-- I remember I had to stand up for-- go represent my school and write-- and recite a poem in Tryncza, like a regional reciting contest. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 10, I believe, somewhere around 10. And she gave me a dress from her daughter. She had a daughter. She gave me this dress to put on because she knew that I would feel very odd in my rather poor dress. And O was always poorly dressed and not too well cared for. And this Chaya was always having something sweet in her house whenever I came. And it was-- and I picked my, my friend Helen to school. We were going to school. And one thing that I was also sort of upset about because my association with Helen already put the blame on me-'s put a black spot on me, because Helen was a Jewish domestic's daughter. But I didn't care. And I loved Helen very much. And with her, we both went to the-- to the camps together. Not that she was there, but-- because we were separated lately-- later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: I would like to come back a little later to Helen and to Chaya and ask you just a little bit more about where you lived in Glogowiec. Can you describe the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the house was sort of a log house, like you have seen quite a few of those probably in Europe-- and whitewashed. And it had one big bedroom and big kitchen. And that was all. In this-- in this one big bedroom, this big bedroom was meant for my aunts. And I had a bed either on the special oven that I don't know whether you would-- you would consider this an oven. It's made of bricks, and it has an elevation where you can sleep under the-- the fire is going at the bottom. And ups-- upstairs, you had to climb on that oven. And you can sleep on those bricks. So I usually slept on top of it. And sometimes I had-- I had two accidents where I fell off. That's about five feet down. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a hot place to sleep?

speaker: interviewee
text: And a hot place to sleep and a hard one, hard one as well. My grandmother slept also in the kitchen. She had a big bed. And sometimes when I couldn't stay-- couldn't really stand the heat-- as they say, if you can't stand the heat, you [LAUGHS] go away. So I went into my grandmother's bed. My brother sl-- and my uncle, they slept on-- in the attic where there was hay, in the hay.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you had spare moments to yourself, what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my spare moments , if I had any, I mostly-- I-- at school, I was studying. And even on-- on my-- on our breaks, I was writing. I was already known there as a little poet. And at after school, we had to work. I had to take care of the cattle. Sometime the cattle-- we had to, first of all before going to school, you had to drive the cattle to the pasture. Sometimes you had to drive the cattle into the-- not sometimes, but most of the time-- I had to ca-- drive the ca-- cattle into the river because the pasture was past the River Wislok. And you drove this cattle while you, yourself, you went into the water. And you were watching them while they were proceeding to the other side. Or you-- I have also learned how to ride a horse. And I was almost like a cowboy on a horse. And I, I have a-- had gone quite a few races with Helen and other boys on the horses on horseback and without a saddle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any dreams when you were little about things you wanted to do when you grew up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I always wanted-- my only dream was to become a lady. The lady is-- the word lady might not mean nothing here. But in Poland-- in that little corner of the world-- when somebody was a teacher or a worker in the office, they called her or him sir or lady. They were-- they had some education. And I wanted to have that education at all price. I wanted to be a professor and also a writer. And for that, whenever I mentioned that word or whenever I managed to drown myself in reading, I, I was beaten. And I was very much scolded for it. And they were telling me, you will never become a lady. You will never become a lady. You'll be always a domestic. You are a good for nothing. It's because you're lazy that you're doing this. And I believe it has happened. And strange as it may be, not long ago-- somewhere in the '70s-- my aunt who was beating me up for saying that I'm going to be a lady titled her letter, my dear lady. So I was quite amused. And it changed my outlook a little bit. I also was drawing a lot. That was another kind of enjoyment that I had in my life wherever I could. And there was-- whenever the river was rising and there was a lot of clay coming with that river, the clay dried. And you had almost tiles of clays cracking. And I was sculpting, making sculptures of that clay while watching the cows and while-- well, or whenever I came from church, and I had the spare time. That was my most, I'd say pleasurable entertainment. Today, you would call this silly. But before, it was before taking care of our TV and other screen shows.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are there any other fond memories from growing up that you recall, perhaps having to do with celebrating religious holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, about the [INAUDIBLE] yes, I could say the most characteristic ones were the third May, third of May. This was a national day for Poland where people, children, were walking to, to church-- children with their teacher. We never really drove. We walked. And it was quite a distance from our village to another because we didn't have a church there. And we enjoyed ourselves singing. That was quite a pleasurable thing. And we had sometimes a few, few meetings with my neighbors and my neighbor children when my people allowed me to do that. I usually was not allowed to play too much. But my family had one thing in common. They all always-- they always sang. And to this day, I remember many, many songs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you sing one?

speaker: interviewee
text: My throat right now is probably too rusty. And I would have to drink. And I don't know whether I could sing. But I'll see. [DRINKS] Well, how about one that is very, very familiar in Polish, OK? This one is rather sad and melancholy. It's called, 'I Am an Orphan.' That's a church song. [SINGING  IN  POLISH] That's on of those. And we had a lot of faster rhythms.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's wonderful. We're just going to-- it's a perfect time. We're going to take a break.
